title: $C^\infty$ closing lemma for three-dimensional Reeb flows via embedded contact homology
speaker: Kei Irie
abstract: $C^r$ closing lemma is an important statement in the theory of dynamical systems, which implies that for a $C^r$ generic system the union of periodic orbits is dense in the nonwondering domain. $C^1$ closing lemma is proved in many classes of dynamical systems, however $C^r$ closing lemma with $r > 1$ is proved only for few cases. In this talk, I'll prove $C^\infty$ closing lemma for Reeb flows on closed contact three-manifolds. The proof uses recent developments in quantitative aspects of embedded contact homology (ECH).

proof_stages.label: Reeb-flow notation and C-infinity closing statement
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines contact forms, Reeb vector fields, periodic Reeb orbits and their union, then states the main perturbation theorem and its C-infinity closing-lemma and generic-density consequences.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness and need for contact methods
proof_stages.summary: Classical C-one/C-two Hamiltonian closing results and Herman's C-infinity counterexample are reviewed to explain why the three-dimensional contact setting requires a genuinely contact-geometric argument.

proof_stages.label: Filtered embedded contact homology and spectral invariants
proof_stages.summary: Filtered ECH is introduced for a nondegenerate closed contact three-manifold, the direct-limit ECH group and Hutchings's spectral invariant are defined, and the key C-zero continuity and spectrality properties are stated.

proof_stages.label: Volume asymptotics imply local sensitivity
proof_stages.summary: The Cristofaro-Gardiner-Hutchings-Ramos volume theorem for ECH spectral invariants is used to deduce that every nontrivial nonnegative conformal change of the contact form strictly increases at least one spectral invariant.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction proof of the Reeb closing lemma
proof_stages.summary: Assuming no perturbed periodic orbit meets the support of the perturbation makes the periodic orbit set and action spectrum independent of the perturbation parameter, so continuity plus spectrality force all spectral invariants to be constant, contradicting local sensitivity.

proof_stages.label: Closed-geodesic application
proof_stages.summary: The main Reeb-flow theorem is applied to the unit cotangent bundle of a closed Riemannian surface to obtain C-infinity-small metric perturbations with a closed geodesic meeting a prescribed open set.

proof_stages.label: Hamiltonian surface diffeomorphism closing result
proof_stages.summary: A second application, joint work, gives a C-infinity closing lemma for Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms of closed symplectic surfaces and places it in the broader problem of generic density for area-preserving maps.

proof_stages.label: Reeb-return-map proof sketch for the surface application
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch perturbs a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism near a contractible fixed point, treats hyperbolic and elliptic locally stable cases separately, realizes a neighborhood of the fixed point as part of a global Reeb return map, and then invokes the Reeb closing lemma.

examples.name: Herman torus Hamiltonian counterexample
examples.note: The Hamiltonian on a higher-dimensional torus, with a Diophantine frequency condition discussed in the subsequent questions, is used to show that a C-infinity closing lemma fails for autonomous Hamiltonian systems and hence that the contact/Reeb hypothesis is genuinely needed.
examples.engagement_reason: the Herman torus example is cited as a known counterexample, with only descriptive facts and conditions stated rather than a derivation carried out

passes.reason: Herman’s torus Hamiltonian is used explicitly to refute the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing lemma, but it is not a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
Herman's Diophantine torus Hamiltonian is a highly regular integrable torus model, deployed explicitly as a counterexample to the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing lemma.
Herman's integrable/translation-regular torus Hamiltonian with Diophantine behavior is used to refute the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing lemma.

It is explicitly used to refute the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing lemma, and its Diophantine torus dynamics are highly regular/integrable.
Herman's integrable torus Hamiltonian is used to refute the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing lemma, and its quasi-periodic torus dynamics is highly regular rather than sporadic or degenerate.
Herman's Diophantine/integrable torus Hamiltonian is explicitly used to refute the C∞ Hamiltonian closing lemma, and its torus-linear dynamics are highly regular.
it is explicitly used to disprove the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing lemma, via a highly regular Diophantine torus/KAM model
Used explicitly to disprove the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing lemma, with a highly regular Diophantine torus Hamiltonian rather than a sporadic or degenerate object.
It is explicitly used to disprove the C-infinity Hamiltonian closing/general-density analogue, while the Diophantine torus construction is systematic rather than sporadic or extremal.

examples.name: Closed geodesics after perturbing a surface metric
examples.note: A closed Riemannian surface is used as an application in which the Reeb closing lemma on the unit cotangent bundle gives a C-infinity-small conformal metric perturbation producing a closed geodesic through a prescribed open set.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives the perturbed closed-geodesic corollary by applying the Reeb closing lemma to the unit cotangent bundle and explains how metric perturbations affect the geodesic flow

passes.reason: The perturbed-surface closed geodesic is presented as a positive application of the closing lemma and is otherwise an ordinary nonconstant closed geodesic.
The perturbed-metric closed geodesic is an ordinary nonconstant periodic geodesic used as a positive application of the closing lemma.
The perturbed surface metric produces an ordinary nonconstant closed geodesic through a chosen open set as a positive application.
The closed geodesic for a small perturbation of a surface metric is a positive application of the theorem and is structurally an ordinary periodic geodesic.
the perturbed surface metric with a resulting nonconstant closed geodesic is a positive application rather than a structurally special obstruction
The perturbed surface metric producing a nonconstant closed geodesic is a positive application for ordinary closed surfaces, not an obstruction.
The perturbed-metric closed geodesic is presented as a positive application of the closing lemma, not as a structurally special obstruction.
The perturbed-metric closed geodesic is a positive application of the Reeb-flow closing lemma and is an ordinary nonconstant closed geodesic on a surface.
The perturbed-metric closed geodesic is a positive application of the closing lemma and is structurally an ordinary closed geodesic.

Presented as a positive application producing an ordinary nonconstant closed geodesic after a small metric perturbation.
The closed geodesic on a small perturbation of a surface metric is a positive application of the closing lemma and is otherwise an ordinary periodic geodesic.

examples.name: Two-sphere area-preserving maps
examples.note: The sphere is singled out as a concrete case where the Hamiltonian-diffeomorphism corollary resolves the general-density problem because every symplectic map of the sphere is Hamiltonian and smooth maps are dense in finite differentiability classes.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the surface Hamiltonian closing result to the two-sphere, using that every symplectic map on S2 is Hamiltonian and density of smooth maps to deduce the area-preserving generic-density statement

passes.reason: The two-sphere case is the genus-zero/minimal closed surface where area-preserving maps reduce to Hamiltonian ones, used for a positive density result rather than as a counterexample.
The two-sphere case is the genus-zero/minimal closed-surface case, with vanishing H1 making area-preserving maps Hamiltonian, and is used for a positive density result.
The two-sphere is the genus-zero boundary case, with maximal standard symmetry, used to obtain a positive density result for area-preserving maps.
Area-preserving maps of the two-sphere are treated as a positive genus-zero boundary case where every symplectic map is Hamiltonian, not as a counterexample.

The two-sphere is the genus-zero boundary case among closed surfaces and is used to obtain a positive density result, not to refute one.
The two-sphere case is used positively, and S^2 is the genus-zero minimal closed surface with trivial H1, making it a boundary/topologically simple case.
Area-preserving maps on S2 are used for a positive generic-density result, with S2 the genus-zero endpoint where every symplectic map is Hamiltonian.
The two-sphere case is used as a positive special case, with S2 the genus-zero/minimal closed surface and H1=0 so area-preserving maps are Hamiltonian.
